ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FJP (Noodles, cooked) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for spicy-sauce dressed noodles in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226529007 (Noodle dish) is the closest available parent concept. No specific code for Hong You Ran Mian exists.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Noodle with Spicy Sauce is approximately 250 g, equivalent to one restaurant bowl as typically served at Din Tai Fung Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Noodles, wheat, cooked, with oil and sauce — estimated composite from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (noodles, cooked) and FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table analogues for chili-oil dressed noodle dishes
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / FAO INFOODS
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Hong You Ran Mian (red-oil spicy noodle) exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA tables. Nutrient values are estimated by compositing: (1) USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 20110 — Noodles, egg, cooked, enriched (per 100 g: energy 138 kcal, protein 4.5 g, fat 2.1 g, carbohydrate 25.2 g) as the noodle base; (2) chili oil and sesame paste contribution estimated from USDA SR Legacy entries for sesame oil and chili oil; (3) sodium elevated to reflect soy sauce seasoning typical of this dish style. Final composite per-100 g values reflect a dressed, ready-to-eat noodle dish. Confidence is medium due to estimation.

gi_source.title: Noodles, instant, boiled — GI analogous estimate; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Hong You Ran Mian (red-oil spicy noodle). GI of 52 is estimated using wheat noodles (boiled) as the closest analogous food from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283), which reports GI values of 47–58 for boiled wheat noodles. A midpoint of 52 is used. The chili oil and sesame paste dressing is expected to slightly lower the effective GI due to fat content. GL per serving = (52 × 24.5 × 2.5) / 100 = 31.9 g, based on 250 g serving size.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce seasoning (~420 mg per 100 g; ~1050 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its high sodium content (~1050 mg per 250 g serving), which represents approximately 46% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg; requesting reduced soy sauce or consuming a smaller portion is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (52) and high glycemic load per serving (~32 g) from refined wheat noodle base
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be cautious with this dish as the refined wheat noodle base contributes a moderate GI of 52 and a high glycemic load of approximately 32 per serving; portion control to half a bowl and pairing with non-starchy vegetables can help moderate the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate fat content including chili oil and sesame paste
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate intake of this dish due to its high sodium content (~1050 mg per serving) and moderate fat content (~19.5 g per serving, predominantly unsaturated from sesame and chili oil), which may contribute to blood pressure elevation and overall caloric load if consumed frequently.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Noodle with Spicy Sauce (红油燃面, Hóng Yóu Rán Miàn) is a Chinese noodle dish originating from Sichuan cuisine, popularised in Singapore through the Din Tai Fung restaurant chain. The dish consists of springy wheat noodles tossed in a complex, aromatic red chili oil sauce made with sesame paste, soy sauce, garlic, Sichuan peppercorn, vinegar, and sugar. The name 'ran mian' (燃面) literally means 'burning noodle', referencing the fiery heat of the chili oil dressing. The texture is characteristically chewy and slippery, with a bold, layered flavour profile combining spicy, numbing, savoury, and slightly sweet notes. Nutritionally, a 250 g serving provides approximately 463 kcal, 13 g protein, 19.5 g fat, and 61 g carbohydrate. The dish is high in sodium (~1050 mg per serving) due to soy sauce seasoning, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The moderate glycemic index (~52) and high glycemic load (~32) from the refined wheat noodle base require portion awareness for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The fat content is predominantly unsaturated, derived from sesame paste and chili oil. This dish is suitable for vegetarians but is not vegan (may contain egg in noodle), not halal-certified at all outlets, and contains gluten and sesame allergens.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Hong You Ran Mian exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition tables. Nutrient values are estimated by compositing USDA SR Legacy data for cooked wheat noodles with estimated contributions from chili oil, sesame paste, and soy sauce seasoning. GI is estimated from the closest analogous food (boiled wheat noodles) in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated composites; no exact laboratory analysis or approved-source entry for Hong You Ran Mian was found. Values should be updated if HPB Singapore Foodo or a peer-reviewed Singapore food composition study publishes a direct entry for this dish. 2. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy noodle base entry and the chili oil contribution is not quantified in approved sources. 3. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the composite source entries used. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as this dish contains no animal-derived ingredients in its standard formulation and B12 is not reported in the source entries. 5. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy noodle base entry used. 6. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database entries used. 7. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the composite source entries used. 8. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the composite source entries used. 9. trans_fat_g is 0.0 as no hydrogenated fats are used in the standard formulation; confirmed negligible per USDA SR Legacy sesame oil and chili oil entries. 10. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as the standard formulation uses plant-based oils and no animal-derived fat; confirmed negligible per USDA SR Legacy entries for sesame oil. 11. Halal status is uncertain; Din Tai Fung Singapore is not universally halal-certified. 12. The dish may contain egg in the noodle dough depending on the specific noodle type used by Din Tai Fung; is_vegan is set to false accordingly.
